_metadata.description: Aggregate student-workload calibration — the Carnegie credit-hour norm + reading-rate math, and the distribution-vs-volume split. Distribution/density is computable from due dates; reading hours are not (readings are links/files), so volume is a rough sanity note only.
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~3,300 chars / 4 ≈ 825 tokens — 'embed' band, but read_at_runtime per the selective_load pattern shared by the other knowledge files (canvas_course_expert loads a few per task).

scope: How much gradable work a course asks (volume) and how it's distributed across the term (density/clustering). Complements cognitive_load (per-task mental load) with the aggregate budget view. Boundaries: per-task load is cognitive_load; the term schedule is syllabus; this is the aggregate work distribution. Honest scope: distribution computable from due dates; reading hours NOT reliably knowable from the API.

facts.definition: Two questions. VOLUME: does estimated total student time roughly match the credit-hour budget (Carnegie: ~3 total hrs/credit/week, so a 3-credit course ≈ 9 hrs/week)? DISTRIBUTION: is work spread evenly, or does it clump (three deliverables one week, nothing the next)? Clumping is a workload defect even when the total is reasonable.

Computable now from the API: count, type, points, and DUE-DATE distribution of gradable work; week-by-week clustering; points concentration. NOT reliably computable: exact reading HOURS — readings are usually links/file attachments whose word counts the API can't see. So the toolkit audits work DISTRIBUTION/DENSITY confidently and offers only a rough VOLUME sanity note (item count vs a credits-scaled expectation), never a precise hour budget. Precise hour-estimation (Wake Forest estimator) needs reading word-counts a human supplies.
citations: Wake Forest Course Workload Estimator (Barre & Esarey)

facts.definition: uneven_distribution (a week carrying >=2x the term average + >=3 items — the primary reliable finding); front_loaded / back_loaded (work concentrated early = weeder pattern, or piled at the end); volume sanity (item count very high/low vs a credits-scaled band — a prompt, not a verdict); mostly_unscheduled (work exists but has no due dates → distribution unknowable; add dates).

verdict workload ∈ {balanced, uneven, sparse, unscheduled} + flags (uneven_distribution / front_loaded / back_loaded / mostly_unscheduled / low_volume / high_volume). Emitted by workload_audit.py. Reading hours are explicitly not measured.

sources: Carnegie unit / U.S. credit-hour norm (~1 hr instruction + ~2 hrs out-of-class per credit per week).
Course Workload Estimator (Wake Forest; Betsy Barre & Justin Esarey) — estimate student hours from readings + assignments.

validation.status: v0.1 — real-course-validated read-only (ITM327 uneven; sandbox/ds250 balanced), 2026-05-26. Distribution logic is reliable; thresholds are fresh and may refine with more real-course runs. Wired into canvas_course_expert + cataloged.

changes: Created from the PTC full-book deep-dive (new-topic #1, Ch 6 workload thread). Public sources only (Carnegie norm, reading-rate, Wake Forest estimator). Grounds workload_audit.py (distribution/density audit + rough volume sanity). Wired into canvas_course_expert cross_references + knowledge/README catalog.
